title: Where in Your Body You Are Right Now

hook.rwenLine: Most of us live a few inches behind our eyes. The body is downstairs somewhere, sending up complaints. Today we start the slow practice of being in the body, not just sending it instructions.
hook.culturalNote: Interoception — the sense of what is happening inside your own body — predicts emotional regulation, decision-making, and resilience under stress more reliably than IQ. People who can locate themselves in their bodies recover from hard moments faster. The good news is that this is a trainable skill.

chunks.native: The sense of what is happening inside your body — temperature, heartbeat, breath, hunger, tension
chunks.literal: Bud Craig — neuroscience of bodily sensation; Lisa Feldman Barrett — emotion construction
chunks.phonetic: Inner perception. A real sense, like sight or hearing.

chunks.native: The deliberate act of asking 'where am I right now?' and feeling the answer rather than calculating it
chunks.literal: Somatic psychotherapy — orientation as a foundation skill before any other body work
chunks.phonetic: Not 'where should I be?' Where are you actually?

chunks.native: Treating the body as a vehicle rather than as part of you — the default for most adults in office work
chunks.literal: Antonio Damasio — Descartes' Error: we are not minds renting bodies
chunks.phonetic: It is more common than not. It is also costing you.

pattern.name: The body answers if you ask it.
pattern.explanation: If you ask 'how am I?' your mind will give you a story. If you ask 'where am I in my body right now?' your body will give you data. Both are useful. The body data is harder to argue with — your shoulders being up around your ears is a fact in a way that 'I'm a bit stressed' is not. Self-knowledge starts here because the body is closer to the truth than the inner monologue is.

examples.native: My jaw is clenched and my breathing is high in my chest.
My eyes feel heavy and my arms are heavy from the shoulder down.
There's tension across my forehead. My stomach is tight. I'm not fine.

exercises.instruction: Match the head-story to the body data underneath

pairs.right: Jaw clenched, breath high in chest
Eyes heavy, arms heavy from shoulder down

exercises.question: Two people walk into a meeting. One thinks 'I'm fine, just a bit tired.' The other notices their shoulders are pulled up and their breath is shallow. Which is the interoceptive read?

options.text: The one who said 'I'm fine, just a bit tired'
The one who noticed their shoulders and breath
Both — they're the same thing in different words

exercises.explanation: Interoception is the body-level read — shoulders pulled up, breath shallow — not the head-level summary 'I'm fine, just tired'. The body version is more specific and harder to argue with.

exercises.instruction: Rewrite the head-story as a body observation
exercises.prompt: I'm a bit on edge today.
correct: My shoulders are up and my breath is shallow.
My jaw is clenched. My stomach feels tight.
There is tension across my forehead and my hands are cold.

exercises.sentence: When the mind is spinning, the _____ is still here. The senses are the cheapest reliable way back to the present.

exercises.translation: The one-line check that turns interoception on.

exercises.instruction: When does head-only living cost the most?
exercises.question: You're in a difficult meeting. You feel 'fine'. After it ends you have a headache, a tight stomach, and you snap at the next person who asks you something. What just happened?

options.text: The body was sending stress signals through the meeting. The head ignored them. The cost showed up later.
The body randomly decided to feel terrible right after the meeting.
The next person who asked you something was being annoying.

exercises.explanation: Stress signals run through the body in real time. Ignoring them doesn't make them go away — it just delays where they land. Interoception is the early-warning system.

rwenDialogue.intro: Let's practise the locating prompt together. I'll ask the question; you answer with body data rather than a story.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Right now — where are you in your body? Not how you feel. Where are you?

userChoices.target: My shoulders are tight. My breath is shallow. My hands are warm.
userChoices.native: Body-level answer — three pieces of specific data.
userChoices.feedback: That's the move. Three pieces of body data, no story. You can do anything with that. You can't do much with 'I'm fine'.

userChoices.target: I'm okay, just a bit tired.
userChoices.native: Head-level answer — a summary, not data.
userChoices.feedback: That's a head answer. Try again from the body — what is your jaw doing right now? Your stomach? Your breath?

userChoices.native: An honest answer for the first few attempts.
userChoices.feedback: Honest, and common. The skill is in the asking, not the answering. Start with one thing — what's your breath doing right now?

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Notice that body data is harder to argue with than a story. That's the whole point. You can negotiate with 'I'm stressed.' You can't really argue with 'my jaw is clenched.'

activeRecall.instruction: Without looking back — answer from memory.

correct: Where am I in my body right now?
Where am I in my body?

prompts.prompt: Why is body data more useful than head-story?

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Three times today — set a phone reminder if you need to — ask yourself 'where am I in my body right now?' and answer with at least one piece of specific body data. Shoulders. Jaw. Stomach. Breath. One answer is enough. You don't have to fix anything.
mission.rwenSignoff: The body has been there all along. Today we said hello back.

phase8.scenario: User has just done one body-locating check after a stressful moment in their day — a meeting, a difficult message, a parenting flare-up. Rwen helps them name three pieces of body data before they leave the conversation.
phase8.rwenRole: A calm, present companion who asks rather than tells. Doesn't fix, doesn't reassure prematurely — just helps the user notice what is actually in the body right now.
phase8.successCriteria: User has named at least three specific pieces of body data (not stories) about how they feel right now.
